role.name: Project Manager
role.description: Control scope, communication, expectations, and team pressure.

chapters.title: Small Change Sir
chapters.story: Client says, “Small change hai.” Actual change: new login, new dashboard, reports, payment gateway, WhatsApp, AI chatbot, and probably moon landing.

outcome.title: Infinite Sprint Ending
outcome.description: Developer starts living under desk. QA creates a bug called “Project has no ending.”

professionalFeedback.mentorFeedback: Mentor feedback: funny disaster aside, this is a professional risk. Stop, document, and repair the decision path before moving forward.
professionalFeedback.safeExplanation: Use evidence, documentation, and escalation when the risk is bigger than your authority.

choices.text: Prepare impact analysis, timeline, cost, and approval note

outcome.title: Scope Shield Ending
outcome.description: Client understands impact. Team gets realistic timeline. Nobody needs emotional support samosa today.
outcome.moralLesson: A PM protects both client expectations and team capacity.

professionalFeedback.mentorFeedback: Mentor feedback: good judgment. You balanced action with evidence, communication, and professional responsibility.
professionalFeedback.safeExplanation: Use evidence, documentation, and escalation when the risk is bigger than your authority.
professionalFeedback.practicalTakeaway: A PM protects both client expectations and team capacity.

outcome.title: Trust Broken Ending
outcome.description: Team morale drops. Developer updates resume during standup.
outcome.moralLesson: Leadership means ownership, not blame shifting.

professionalFeedback.mentorFeedback: Mentor feedback: funny disaster aside, this is a professional risk. Stop, document, and repair the decision path before moving forward.
professionalFeedback.safeExplanation: Use evidence, documentation, and escalation when the risk is bigger than your authority.
professionalFeedback.practicalTakeaway: Leadership means ownership, not blame shifting.

miniGame.title: Scope Change Decision Matrix
miniGame.instructions: Select the responsible decisions for handling a surprise scope change.
miniGame.prompt: Client says 'small change sir' but the change touches budget, timeline, and three teams.
miniGame.hint: Good PM decisions protect scope, budget, timeline, and communication.

options.text: Prepare impact analysis for timeline and cost
Raise a formal change request for approval
Say yes silently and hope the team forgives you
options.helperText: Team morale has left the chat.

miniGame.successMessage: Scope controlled. The project plan smiled for the first time this sprint.
miniGame.failureMessage: Scope exploded. The Gantt chart has started writing its resignation letter.

chapters.learningObjective: Practice scope triage workflow for a Project Manager situation involving scope creep and communication.
safetyGuardrails: Do not hide risk to protect status color.
Balance scope, time, cost, quality, and people.
chapters.practicalTakeaway: Use the scope triage workflow to balance professional judgment with constraints instead of guessing under pressure.
chapters.safeExplanation: Delivery simulation: do not hide risk; document scope/time/budget trade-offs and escalate blockers early. This chapter is educational and does not replace qualified professional review.
chapters.mentorFeedback: Delivery Mentor says: At beginner level, show your evidence, explain the trade-off, and follow the scope triage workflow before choosing speed.

guardrails: Do not hide risk to protect status color.
Balance scope, time, cost, quality, and people.
safetyReview.notes: Phase 23 metadata seed. Requires human review before production publishing changes.

chapters.title: Standup Storm
chapters.story: Daily standup begins. Developer says “almost done,” QA says “blocked,” client says “urgent,” and the meeting is already 45 minutes old.

choices.text: Let everyone discuss every detail live

outcome.title: Meeting Marathon Ending
outcome.description: Standup becomes sit-down. Team productivity leaves quietly through the back door.
outcome.moralLesson: Standups should surface blockers, not solve every problem live.

professionalFeedback.mentorFeedback: Mentor feedback: funny disaster aside, this is a professional risk. Stop, document, and repair the decision path before moving forward.
professionalFeedback.safeExplanation: Use evidence, documentation, and escalation when the risk is bigger than your authority.
professionalFeedback.practicalTakeaway: Standups should surface blockers, not solve every problem live.

choices.text: Capture blockers, assign owners, park deep discussions, and confirm next steps

outcome.title: Focused PM Ending
outcome.description: The meeting ends on time, blockers have owners, and nobody has to fake network issues.
outcome.moralLesson: Good meetings create clarity and action.

professionalFeedback.mentorFeedback: Mentor feedback: acceptable start, but explain the trade-off and document what you checked.
professionalFeedback.safeExplanation: Use evidence, documentation, and escalation when the risk is bigger than your authority.
professionalFeedback.practicalTakeaway: Good meetings create clarity and action.

outcome.title: Mystery Sprint Ending
outcome.description: Nobody knows status until delivery day. Delivery day files a missing person report.
outcome.moralLesson: Process should be improved, not abandoned blindly.

professionalFeedback.mentorFeedback: Mentor feedback: funny disaster aside, this is a professional risk. Stop, document, and repair the decision path before moving forward.
professionalFeedback.safeExplanation: Use evidence, documentation, and escalation when the risk is bigger than your authority.
professionalFeedback.practicalTakeaway: Process should be improved, not abandoned blindly.

chapters.learningObjective: Practice risk register workflow for a Project Manager situation involving team communication and blockers.
safetyGuardrails: Do not hide risk to protect status color.
Balance scope, time, cost, quality, and people.
chapters.practicalTakeaway: Use the risk register workflow to balance professional judgment with constraints instead of guessing under pressure.
chapters.safeExplanation: Delivery simulation: do not hide risk; document scope/time/budget trade-offs and escalate blockers early. This chapter is educational and does not replace qualified professional review.
chapters.mentorFeedback: Delivery Mentor says: At intermediate level, show your evidence, explain the trade-off, and follow the risk register workflow before choosing speed.

guardrails: Do not hide risk to protect status color.

chapters.title: Demo Day Disaster
chapters.story: Demo is in one hour. Build is ready, but nobody has checked test data, permissions, or the projector. Classic suspense.

outcome.title: Live Demo Ghost Ending
outcome.description: Login fails, test data is empty, and the projector shows only your desktop wallpaper.
outcome.moralLesson: Demo readiness must be verified, not hoped for.

professionalFeedback.mentorFeedback: Mentor feedback: funny disaster aside, this is a professional risk. Stop, document, and repair the decision path before moving forward.
professionalFeedback.safeExplanation: Use evidence, documentation, and escalation when the risk is bigger than your authority.
professionalFeedback.practicalTakeaway: Demo readiness must be verified, not hoped for.

choices.text: Run a dry test, verify data, roles, flow, fallback, and speaking points

outcome.title: Confident Demo Ending
outcome.description: The demo runs smoothly, questions are handled, and the team gets actual appreciation.

professionalFeedback.mentorFeedback: Mentor feedback: acceptable start, but explain the trade-off and document what you checked.
professionalFeedback.safeExplanation: Use evidence, documentation, and escalation when the risk is bigger than your authority.

choices.text: Ask developer to demo without context

outcome.title: Developer Poetry Ending
outcome.description: Developer explains database indexes while client asks where the button is.

professionalFeedback.mentorFeedback: Mentor feedback: funny disaster aside, this is a professional risk. Stop, document, and repair the decision path before moving forward.
professionalFeedback.safeExplanation: Use evidence, documentation, and escalation when the risk is bigger than your authority.
